terms.definition: A rapid, system-wide loss of functional integrity arising from internal stresses that exceed adaptive capacity
A social condition in which institutions and strangers are expected to act predictably and in good faith
The capacity of technological artefacts to grant individuals discretionary control over domains previously regulated by external authority
The ability to separate sexual activity from procreation, allowing voluntary control over fertility
The pattern by which moral norms regulate economic exchange and obligations
The socially recognized right to define normative boundaries and adjudicate behavior
Aggregate belief that institutions act competently and benevolently
Schooling saturation above two-thirds of an age cohort, producing widespread statistical literacy and ideological reflexivity
A nonlinear qualitative change arising when a quantitative parameter crosses a critical threshold
An artefact whose dominant externality is the realignment of normative structures
The transfer of normative arbitration from sacred to empirical or procedural discourse
A state of communication technology penetration exceeding 85% of households, enabling synchronized national attention
Perceived rightful authority of an institution or rule system
Institutional pursuit of equitable distribution of resources, rights, and opportunities
Policy aim in which end-state distributions, not merely opportunities, are equalized across populations
